name: CJC-1295
summary: CJC-1295 is a synthetic analog of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) based on the GRF(1-29) sequence, designed to stimulate the pituitary to release growth hormone (GH) and raise IGF-1. It exists in two forms: 'with DAC' (a Drug Affinity Complex that binds serum albumin for a multi-day half-life and weekly dosing) and 'without DAC' (Modified GRF 1-29, a short-acting version dosed daily that better preserves natural pulsatile GH release).
mechanism:
CJC-1295 is a tetrasubstituted analog of the first 29 amino acids of GHRH (GRF 1-29, the bioactive core also known as sermorelin). The amino-acid substitutions protect the peptide from rapid enzymatic degradation by dipeptidyl peptidase-IV (DPP-IV), extending its activity relative to native GHRH. It acts on GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs to increase the amplitude of growth hormone pulses, which in turn raises hepatic IGF-1 production.

The defining structural feature of the 'with DAC' version is a maleimidopropionyl-lysine (Drug Affinity Complex) moiety that forms a covalent bond with circulating serum albumin. This dramatically prolongs circulation, giving an estimated half-life of roughly 6-8 days and producing sustained, tonic elevation of GH and IGF-1. The 'without DAC' version (Modified GRF 1-29) lacks the albumin-binding complex, so it has a short half-life of about 30 minutes and produces a brief, more physiologic GH pulse.

Because GHRH analogs and GH-releasing peptides (GHRPs, e.g. ipamorelin) act through different receptors and complementary pathways, they are frequently studied as a combination to amplify GH release beyond either compound alone.
benefits: Stimulates endogenous GH release and raises IGF-1 rather than supplying exogenous GH
In a controlled trial, single doses raised mean plasma GH 2- to 10-fold for 6+ days and IGF-1 1.5- to 3-fold for 9-11 days (DAC version)
Multiple DAC doses kept IGF-1 elevated for up to ~28 days in healthy adults
Studied for body composition support (lean mass, fat metabolism), recovery, and sleep quality via the GH axis
Without-DAC (Mod GRF 1-29) form better preserves the natural pulsatile pattern of GH secretion
Synergizes with GHRPs such as ipamorelin for a larger combined GH pulse
risks: Not FDA-approved for human use; clinical development was abandoned and there is no approved therapeutic product
Prohibited at all times by WADA (GH secretagogues / GHRH class) in competitive sport
Sustained IGF-1 elevation from the DAC form carries theoretical concern regarding tissue/tumor growth and insulin resistance

Manufacturer trials were discontinued after a participant death in 2006 (attributed by the attending physician to pre-existing coronary disease, not the drug); long-term human safety is not established
Much enthusiast dosing guidance is extrapolated; rigorous long-term human safety/efficacy data are limited
Quality and purity of research-grade material vary; contamination risk with non-pharmaceutical sources
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions: redness, itching, swelling, or pain
Facial flushing and transient warmth shortly after injection
Joint aches or carpal-tunnel-like symptoms with excessive GH/IGF-1 elevation
Reduced insulin sensitivity / higher blood glucose with sustained high IGF-1

dosage.notes: Common educational protocols differ sharply by form. WITH DAC: a frequently cited research dose is ~1-2 mg per week (often split into 1-2 injections) because the multi-day half-life provides tonic stimulation; clinical trials studied 30, 60, and 125 mcg/kg single doses. WITHOUT DAC (Mod GRF 1-29): ~100 mcg per dose, 1-3x daily, often timed at bedtime and/or post-workout to match natural GH pulses, frequently combined 1:1 with ipamorelin (e.g. 100 mcg + 100 mcg). The numeric range above reflects the without-DAC per-dose model; for the DAC form think in mg/week instead.

frequency: Without DAC (Mod GRF 1-29): daily, often 1-3x/day (e.g. bedtime, post-workout). With DAC: roughly once or twice weekly.
halfLife: Without DAC (Mod GRF 1-29): ~30 minutes. With DAC: ~6-8 days due to covalent albumin binding.
cycleLength: Commonly studied/used in 8-12 week cycles; longer GH-axis use is sometimes cycled with breaks to monitor IGF-1.

reconstitution.notes: 2 mg vial + 2 mL bacteriostatic water = 1 mg/mL (1000 mcg/mL), so 100 mcg = 0.1 mL (10 units on a U-100 insulin syringe) and 1 mg = 1.0 mL (100 units). Add the diluent slowly down the vial wall and swirl gently; do not shake. Adjust diluent volume to make per-dose measurement convenient.

storage: Store lyophilized vials refrigerated (2-8C) or frozen and protected from light. After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2-8C and use within ~3-4 weeks; do not freeze the reconstituted solution.
contraindications: Active or history of cancer / malignancy (GH-IGF-1 axis stimulation is a theoretical growth concern)
Diabetes or significant insulin resistance without close monitoring (IGF-1 can impair glucose control)
Competitive athletes subject to anti-doping testing (WADA-prohibited)

interactions.note: Classic GHRH + GHRP combination. CJC-1295 (a GHRH analog) increases GH pulse amplitude while ipamorelin (a selective GH-releasing peptide/ghrelin-receptor agonist) triggers a GH pulse via a separate receptor; together they produce a larger, more reliable GH release than either alone. The without-DAC (Mod GRF 1-29) form is most often paired 1:1 with ipamorelin.

Tesamorelin is also a GHRH analog acting on the same pituitary GHRH receptors. Stacking two GHRH analogs is redundant rather than additive and compounds GH/IGF-1 elevation, increasing the risk of fluid retention, joint symptoms, and reduced insulin sensitivity; combining them offers little benefit over choosing one.

Non-overlapping pathways: CJC-1295 drives the GH/IGF-1 axis for anabolism and recovery, while MOTS-c acts on mitochondrial/metabolic (AMPK) signaling. Sometimes combined in body-composition-focused research protocols, though human evidence is limited.

references.title: Teichman et al. Prolonged stimulation of GH and IGF-I secretion by CJC-1295, a long-acting analog of GHRH, in healthy adults (PubMed, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2006)
Prolonged Stimulation of GH and IGF-I Secretion by CJC-1295 (Oxford Academic / JCEM)
Activation of the GH/IGF-1 axis by CJC-1295, a long-acting GHRH analog (PubMed)
